title: Local multiferroic ordering at room temperature in collinear magnetoelectric antiferromagnets induced by flexo-Zeeman coupling
authors: Paulina J. Prusik, Igor Veremchuk, Florin Radu, Andrey N. Anisimov, Pavlo Makushko, Georgy V. Astakhov, René Hübner, Alexander Edström, Jürgen Fassbender, Fabian Ganss, Massimiliano Stengel, Kirill D. Belashchenko, Denys Makarov, Oleksandr V. Pylypovskyi
abstract: Spin-driven multiferroicity attracts significant interest due to its tunability and inherently strong magnetoelectric coupling. While this mechanism induces sizeable electric polarization, it typically occurs at low temperatures and in complex materials. In the simple oxide, the magnetoelectric antiferromagnet Cr$_2$O$_3$, we experimentally demonstrate the existence of specific domain walls that act as room-temperature multiferroic regions. This behavior stems from an anisotropic crystal-symmetry-dependent mechanism of exchange origin, which is applicable to a broad class of bipartite antiferromagnets. The key signature is the magnetization occurring at antiferromagnetic textures, driven by the flexo-Zeeman interaction. These findings establish a foundation for exploring high-temperature spin-driven multiferroicity for magnetoelectric spin-orbit memory and logic applications.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the simple antiferromagnet $	ext{Cr}_2	ext{O}_3$ exhibits room-temperature multiferroic behavior localized at its domain walls. This effect is driven by a specific coupling mechanism called the flexo-Zeeman interaction. The findings establish a pathway for developing high-temperature, spin-driven magnetoelectric memory and logic devices.
main_problem: The research aims to demonstrate and understand room-temperature multiferroic ordering in simple, collinear magnetoelectric antiferromagnets.
main_result: The study experimentally and theoretically confirms that domain walls in $	ext{Cr}_2	ext{O}_3$ exhibit multiferroic behavior driven by the flexo-Zeeman interaction, leading to measurable electric polarization and magnetization.
method: The work combines experimental characterization using SNVM and KPFM with theoretical modeling involving the flexo-Zeeman Hamiltonian and *ab initio* calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is the simple oxide $	ext{Cr}_2	ext{O}_3$, an antiferromagnet. The key theoretical element is the flexo-Zeeman coupling term, which links crystal symmetry, Néel vector gradients, and magnetization, leading to non-collinear magnetoelectric effects.
key_observables: Electric polarization ($P$), uncompensated magnetization ($M$), stray field patterns (from SNVM), and the ratio of magnetic transition fields ($	ext{H}_{	ext{DW}}/	ext{H}_{	ext{SF}}$).
important_parameters: Room temperature, flexo-Zeeman coupling coefficient ($b$), and domain wall periodicity ($\approx 300-390 	ext{ nm}$).
main_assumptions: The multiferroicity is localized to specific domain wall orientations in the basal plane, and the effect can be extended to global reconfigurable channels.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the symmetry classification of the flexo-Zeeman interaction, energy dependence of polarization, and comparative SNVM/KPFM scans showing stripe stray fields in domain walls under different magnetic field cooling conditions.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical framework of the flexo-Zeeman coupling, to presenting experimental measurements on domain walls in $	ext{Cr}_2	ext{O}_3$ using multiple techniques, and finally confirming the predicted magnetoelectric response and its implications for device applications.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the study of emergent electric polarization driven by spin textures (domain walls) provides a model for coupling magnetic order parameters to electric polarization, which is a key concept in advanced quantum materials and spintronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Flexo-Zeeman interaction in antiferromagnets. (a) Classification of the symmetry operations within crystal- lographic point groups that allow for the flexo-Zeeman term with ∂in, i = x, y, z. The operations indicated in the white background do not forbid the flexo-Zeeman coupling. (b–c) Energy dependence on polarization: for M ≡0 (b), the minimum is at P = 0; otherwise (c), it corresponds to a spatially localized magnetoelectric multiferroic state with finite polarization. (d) Hexagonal cell of chromia that supports flexo-Zeeman coupling M ∝∂n/∂z. (e,f) Schematic difference between two AFM DWs with different spatial orientation and associated phenomena: the presence or absence of...

FIG. 2. Multiferroic response at domain walls in m-plane chromia at room temperature. (a) SNVM and (b) KPFM scans of the m-plane thin film after field cooling to set the dominant AFM domain when signatures of domain walls or polarization are absent. Scale bar is 0.5 µm. FC stands for field cooling. (c) SNVM scan of the m-plane thin film after zero-field cooling to set a multidomain AFM state. Scale bar is 0.5 µm. Colorbar is shared with panel (a). Representative line profiles of DWs shown in panels (d–f) are indicated with vertical solid lines. The corresponding profiles of the N´eel vector n are shown in panels (G–I). FIG. 3. Quantification of the strength of the flexo-Zeeman interaction in c-plane chromia. (a) Energy of different magnetic states as a function of the magnetic field for a 10ℓ-thick film. Lines and symbols correspond to the analytics and spin-lattice simulations, respectively. (b) Schematics of the collinear state by n (shown by double arrows). (c) Schematics of the domain wall state with the gradient along z-direction. (d) Schematics of the widening of the domain wall with an increase of magnetic field.
